source: Adil Salahi, Muhammad: Man and Prophet, p. 65

story_context.introduction: In the early days of Islam, some people who believed in God were treated very cruelly because of their faith. Many of them were slaves who had no one to protect them. But one kind and wealthy man named Abu Bakr decided to help them.
story_context.conclusion: Abu Bakr freed seven slaves who were being hurt for believing in God. He did not do it for thanks or protection. He did it only to please God. His kindness showed that in Islam, every person is equal and precious.
story_context.moral_lesson: True kindness means helping those in need without expecting anything in return.

panels.unit_title: A man who wanted to help
panels.narrative_text: Abu Bakr was a kind and respected man in Makkah. It made him very sad to see people being hurt simply because they believed in God. He decided he would do something to help them.
panels.moral_lesson: Noticing the suffering of others and choosing to act.
panels.image_prompt: Children's watercolor storybook illustration in a soft, warm, hand-painted style for ages 6-12, with gentle brushstrokes, rounded forms and a tender picture-book quality. The scene is set in a sunlit courtyard of 7th century Makkah, enclosed by warm sandy mud-brick walls with a slender date palm and a clay water jar nearby. Abu Bakr, a slender man in his forties with a thin greying beard and a gentle, determined face, stands at the centre wearing a clean white thobe with a light tan cloak over one shoulder and a white turban, one hand resting thoughtfully over his heart as he gazes outward with quiet compassion. The mud-brick homes of the city rise softly behind him. The mood is gentle and resolute, showing a kind man moved to help those who suffer. Warm golden morning light falls across the courtyard, casting soft long shadows and a serene glow. The composition is a calm medium shot at eye level, Abu Bakr centred and framed by the simple warm architecture. The colour palette blends warm sand, cream, soft amber and gentle brown with a light tan accent, conveying warmth, sincerity and quiet determination. No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

characters.name: Abu Bakr
characters.appearance: a slender man in his forties with a thin greying beard, gentle determined face, wearing a clean white thobe with a light tan cloak and a white turban

compliance.notes: Reviewed and corrected for historical accuracy

panels.narrative_text: The first person Abu Bakr helped was Amir ibn Fuhayrah, a gentle shepherd who was being mistreated for his faith. Abu Bakr paid his master and set him free. Amir later helped the Prophet on his great journey to Madinah.
panels.moral_lesson: Helping others find freedom and dignity.
panels.image_prompt: Children's watercolor storybook illustration in a soft, warm, hand-painted style for ages 6-12, with gentle brushstrokes, rounded forms and a tender picture-book quality. The scene is set in a bustling 7th century Makkah marketplace of mud-brick buildings and date palms, with simple woven awnings and baskets of dates on the warm dusty ground. Abu Bakr, a slender man in his forties with a thin greying beard, a gentle determined face, a clean white thobe, light tan cloak and white turban, stands and gently rests a hand on the shoulder of Amir ibn Fuhayrah, a humble young shepherd with a kind face and short beard wearing a plain brown tunic, who looks up with visible relief and gratitude. Around them a few modestly dressed townsfolk go about their day. The mood is hopeful and warm, capturing the moment a man is set free with dignity. Bright warm sunlight bathes the scene in honey tones, casting soft gentle shadows. The composition is a medium shot at eye level, the two figures clearly shown with their faces visible at the heart of the frame. The colour palette uses warm sand, cream, golden brown and the shepherd's earthy brown tunic, conveying hope, kindness and new freedom. No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.narrative_text: A slave woman named Zunayrah was treated so harshly that she lost her sight. Her tormentor mocked her, but she trusted that God could help her. The next morning, her sight returned. Abu Bakr hurried to set her free.
panels.moral_lesson: Patience and trust in God through hardship.
panels.image_prompt: Children's watercolor storybook illustration in a soft, warm, hand-painted style for ages 6-12, with gentle brushstrokes, rounded forms and a tender picture-book quality. The scene is set in a quiet street of 7th century Makkah lined with warm mud-brick houses and a slender date palm, the dusty ground glowing in soft light. Abu Bakr, a slender man in his forties with a thin greying beard, gentle determined face, clean white thobe, light tan cloak and white turban, stands respectfully beside Zunayrah, a woman fully and modestly dressed in a long flowing earth-toned gown with a head covering completely covering her hair, her expression calm, patient and hopeful as warm golden morning light falls gently on her face. The mood is tender and uplifting, showing patience and trust in God rewarded with relief. Soft warm morning light washes over the quiet street, casting gentle shadows and nothing frightening. The composition is a medium shot at eye level, the two dignified figures with faces visible, framed by the calm street. The colour palette blends warm sand, cream, soft amber and gentle earth tones, conveying patience, hope and quiet grace. No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.narrative_text: Abu Bakr freed another woman, al-Nahdiyah, and her daughter. When their mistress said she would never free them, Abu Bakr paid for them himself. He freed seven people in all who were suffering for their faith.
panels.moral_lesson: Generosity that asks for nothing back.
panels.image_prompt: Children's watercolor storybook illustration in a soft, warm, hand-painted style for ages 6-12, with gentle brushstrokes, rounded forms and a tender picture-book quality. The scene is set inside a modest 7th century Makkah home with mud-brick walls in warm sandy tones, a woven palm mat on the floor, a low wooden table holding a bowl of pale flour, and a clay oil lamp glowing softly. Abu Bakr, a slender man in his forties with a thin greying beard, gentle face, clean white thobe, light tan cloak and white turban, holds out a small cloth pouch to an older woman, while nearby stand al-Nahdiyah and her daughter, both women fully and modestly dressed in long grey-blue gowns with head coverings, their faces showing relief and quiet gratitude. The mood is warm and hopeful, capturing generosity that asks for nothing in return. Soft golden lamplight fills the room, casting gentle flickering shadows on the walls. The composition is a balanced medium shot at eye level, the exchange of the pouch at the centre of the frame. The colour palette uses warm amber, cream, soft brown and gentle grey-blue in the women's gowns, conveying kindness, relief and the quiet dignity of freedom given freely. No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.narrative_text: Abu Bakr's father did not understand. He asked, 'Why free weak slaves instead of strong men who could protect you?' Abu Bakr smiled and answered that he wanted only one thing: to be rewarded by God.
panels.moral_lesson: Doing good purely to please God.
panels.image_prompt: Children's watercolor storybook illustration in a soft, warm, hand-painted style for ages 6-12, with gentle brushstrokes, rounded forms and a tender picture-book quality. The scene is set inside Abu Bakr's 7th century Makkah home, with warm mud-brick walls, a woven floor mat, simple wooden shelves and a clay oil lamp giving a soft glow. Two men sit facing each other on low cushions. Abu Bakr's father, an elderly man with a long white beard and a puzzled, questioning expression, wears tan and grey robes with a white head cloth and leans forward as he speaks. Across from him, Abu Bakr, a slender man in his forties with a thin greying beard, a clean white thobe, light tan cloak and white turban, answers calmly with a gentle, sincere smile and an open hand gesture, his face serene and certain. The mood is warm and thoughtful, capturing a son explaining that he acts only to please God. Soft golden lamplight bathes the room, casting gentle shadows and an intimate atmosphere. The composition is a medium close shot at eye level, the two seated figures forming a quiet, conversational pairing. The colour palette blends warm amber, cream, soft brown and muted grey, conveying sincerity, warmth and gentle wisdom. No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.narrative_text: Among those Abu Bakr freed was Bilal, who would become a beloved companion of the Prophet. Years later, people would say of Abu Bakr: he is our master, and he freed our master.
panels.moral_lesson: Every person is equal and precious before God.
panels.image_prompt: Children's watercolor storybook illustration in a soft, warm, hand-painted style for ages 6-12, with gentle brushstrokes, rounded forms and a tender picture-book quality. The scene is set in a warm sunlit open space in 7th century Makkah, the mud-brick city rising softly behind in honey and sand tones beneath a clear sky. Two men stand side by side as equals. Abu Bakr, a slender man in his forties with a thin greying beard, gentle dignified face, clean white thobe, light tan cloak and white turban, stands beside Bilal, a tall, strong man with a noble calm face, short dark hair and an off-white tunic, both standing upright with quiet dignity and mutual respect. The mood is uplifting and noble, celebrating the bond of brotherhood and the equality of all people. Bright warm daylight with a high golden sun lights the scene, casting soft gentle shadows on the dusty ground. The composition is a medium shot at eye level, the two figures balanced and equal in the frame with their faces visible. The colour palette combines warm sand, cream, golden brown and soft off-white, conveying dignity, equality, respect and the warmth of true companionship. No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.
